approaches.name: Approach 1: Trial Division Brute Force O(sqrt(n))
approaches.summary: Iterate from 2 to sqrt(n), dividing n by each integer while it's divisible to find prime factors. This is straightforward and works for small numbers but inefficient for large inputs.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(1) if output is not counted, else O(k) for k factors

ladders.title: Full Problem — Prime Factorization via Trial Division
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of prime factorization using trial division from 2 to sqrt(n).
ladders.explanation: The trial division method for prime factorization leverages the fundamental theorem of arithmetic, which states every integer greater than 1 can be uniquely represented as a product of prime numbers. The intuition is that if a number n is divisible by a prime p, then p must be a factor, and we can repeatedly divide n by p until it's no longer divisible, extracting all occurrences of p. We only need to check divisors up to sqrt(n) because if n has a factor greater than sqrt(n), it must have a corresponding factor less than sqrt(n) (since factors come in pairs). After the loop, if n remains greater than 1, it's a prime factor itself. The algorithm: initialize an empty list for factors, iterate i from 2 to sqrt(n), while n is divisible by i, add i to factors and divide n by i, increment i. Finally, if n > 1, add n to factors. Time complexity is O(sqrt(n)) in the worst case (when n is prime), and space is O(k) for k factors. This connects directly to the key subroutine by implementing the complete factorization logic. Common mistakes include not handling the leftover n > 1, inefficiently checking all numbers instead of incrementing properly, and incorrect sqrt boundary. Real code pattern: def prime_factors(n): factors = []; i = 2; while i * i <= n: while n % i == 0: factors.append(i); n //= i; i += 1; if n > 1: factors.append(n); return factors.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Extracting All Occurrences of a Divisor
ladders.desc: Repeatedly divide n by a given divisor while it's divisible, collecting factors.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of trial division: given a number n and a divisor d, we repeatedly check if n is divisible by d, and if so, add d to the factor list and update n by integer division. The intuition is that if d divides n, then d is a prime factor (in the context of the full algorithm where d is prime or will be skipped if composite), and we need to extract all powers of d (e.g., for n=8 and d=2, we get three factors of 2). This ensures each factor is recorded as many times as it appears. The algorithm: while n % d == 0: append d to factors, set n = n // d. Time complexity is O(log_d(n)) in the worst case (when n is a power of d), and space is O(k) for factors added. This feeds into the full problem by being called for each potential divisor in the loop. Common mistakes include using floating-point division or not updating n correctly. Real code pattern: while n % d == 0: factors.append(d); n //= d.

examples.trace: Start: n=27, d=3, factors=[]. Check: 27%3=0? Yes. Add 3: factors=[3], n=27//3=9. Check: 9%3=0? Yes. Add 3: factors=[3,3], n=9//3=3. Check: 3%3=0? Yes. Add 3: factors=[3,3,3], n=3//3=1. Check: 1%3=1, no. Stop. Return factors=[3,3,3], n=1.
Start: n=14, d=7, factors=[]. Check: 14%7=0? Yes. Add 7: factors=[7], n=14//7=2. Check: 2%7=2, no. Stop. Return factors=[7], n=2.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Iterating Divisors Up to sqrt(n)
ladders.desc: Looping from 2 to sqrt(n) to find potential prime factors.
ladders.explanation: The central insight is that to find all prime factors of n, we only need to test divisors up to the square root of n. Intuition: if n = a * b, then at least one of a or b must be ≤ sqrt(n) (otherwise a*b > n). Thus, by checking all integers from 2 to sqrt(n), we can identify all small factors; any remaining n > 1 after the loop must be a prime factor itself (the large factor). This reduces the time complexity from O(n) to O(sqrt(n)). The algorithm: for i from 2 to sqrt(n) (inclusive), perform operations. In practice, we use while i*i <= n to avoid floating-point issues. Time complexity is O(sqrt(n)) for the loop, and space is O(1) for the iterator. This connects to the key sub-routine by providing the divisors to test. Common mistakes include using i <= sqrt(n) with floating-point sqrt, which can cause off-by-one errors, or not handling the leftover n. Real code pattern: i = 2; while i * i <= n: # do something; i += 1.

examples.trace: Start: n=36, i=2. Check: i*i=4 <=36? Yes, process i=2. i=3: 9<=36? Yes, process i=3. i=4: 16<=36? Yes, process i=4. i=5: 25<=36? Yes, process i=5. i=6: 36<=36? Yes, process i=6. i=7: 49<=36? No, stop. i values tested: 2,3,4,5,6.
Start: n=17, i=2. Check: i*i=4 <=17? Yes, process i=2. i=3: 9<=17? Yes, process i=3. i=4: 16<=17? Yes, process i=4. i=5: 25<=17? No, stop. i values tested: 2,3,4.

ladders.title: Building Block — Checking Divisibility
ladders.desc: Determining if one integer is divisible by another.
ladders.explanation: Divisibility is the foundational operation for trial division: we need to check if n is divisible by d, meaning n % d == 0 (i.e., the remainder is zero). Intuition: if d divides n evenly, then d is a factor of n. This is based on the definition of division: n = d * q + r, with 0 ≤ r < d; if r=0, then n is divisible by d. In the context of prime factorization, we use this to identify potential prime factors. The algorithm is a simple conditional: if n % d == 0, then divisible. Time complexity is O(1), and space is O(1). This feeds into the core logic by being the condition for the inner loop. Common mistakes include confusing / (float division) with // (integer division) or % (modulo), or not handling edge cases like d=0. Real code pattern: if n % d == 0: # divisible.

examples.trace: Compute n % d: 10 % 2 = 0. Since remainder is 0, 10 is divisible by 2. Return True.
Compute n % d: 7 % 3 = 1. Since remainder is 1 (not 0), 7 is not divisible by 3. Return False.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Integer Division and Modulo
ladders.desc: Performing integer division (//) and modulo (%) operations.
ladders.explanation: Integer division (//) returns the quotient of dividing two integers, discarding any remainder, while modulo (%) returns the remainder. Intuition: these operations are essential for updating n during factorization (using //) and checking divisibility (using %). For example, if n=10 and d=3, 10 // 3 = 3 and 10 % 3 = 1. In trial division, we use n //= d to reduce n after extracting a factor, and n % d to test if d divides n. The algorithm involves simple arithmetic: quotient = n // d, remainder = n % d. Time and space are O(1). This connects to the building block by providing the tools for divisibility checks. Common mistakes include using / which gives a float in Python, leading to precision issues, or misunderstanding negative numbers (but in factorization, we deal with positives). Real code pattern: quotient = n // d; remainder = n % d.

examples.trace: Compute integer division: 10 // 3 = 3 (since 3*3=9 ≤10). Compute modulo: 10 % 3 = 1 (since 10 - 3*3 = 1). Return quotient=3, remainder=1.
Compute integer division: 8 // 2 = 4 (since 4*2=8). Compute modulo: 8 % 2 = 0 (since 8 - 4*2 = 0). Return quotient=4, remainder=0.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Prime Numbers and Factors
ladders.desc: Understanding prime numbers and what it means for a number to be a factor.
ladders.explanation: A prime number is a natural number greater than 1 that has no positive divisors other than 1 and itself (e.g., 2, 3, 5, 7). A factor of a number n is an integer that divides n without leaving a remainder (e.g., factors of 6 are 1, 2, 3, 6). The intuition for prime factorization is that every composite number can be broken down uniquely into a product of primes, which are its building blocks. For example, 12 = 2 * 2 * 3. This is based on the fundamental theorem of arithmetic. In trial division, we aim to find these prime factors by testing divisibility. Time and space are not applicable here as it's conceptual. This feeds into basic operations by defining what we're computing. Common mistakes include confusing prime with odd (2 is prime but even) or thinking 1 is prime (it's not). Real code pattern: is_prime(n): if n < 2: return False; for i in range(2, int(sqrt(n))+1): if n % i == 0: return False; return True.

examples.trace: Check if 7 is prime: divisors of 7 are 1 and 7 only, so it's prime. Factors of 7: numbers that divide 7 evenly: 1 and 7. So prime=True, factors=[1,7].
Check if 10 is prime: divisors include 2 and 5 (besides 1 and 10), so it's not prime. Factors of 10: numbers that divide 10 evenly: 1, 2, 5, 10. So prime=False, factors=[1,2,5,10].

approaches.name: Approach 2: Optimized Trial Division with Odds O(sqrt(n))
approaches.summary: After handling factor 2, iterate only over odd numbers from 3 to sqrt(n) to reduce checks. This improves constant factors but still relies on trial division.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(1) if output is not counted, else O(k) for k factors

ladders.title: Full Problem — Prime Factorization via Optimized Trial Division
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of prime factorization using optimized trial division with odd numbers only after handling factor 2.
ladders.explanation: This approach factorizes a given integer n into its prime factors using an optimized version of trial division. The intuition is that after handling the factor 2 (the only even prime), we only need to check odd divisors from 3 up to sqrt(n), since any composite number must have a prime factor ≤ sqrt(n). This reduces the number of checks by approximately half compared to checking all numbers up to sqrt(n). The algorithm works by repeatedly dividing n by its smallest prime factor until n becomes 1. For each divisor d, we check if n is divisible by d. If yes, d is a prime factor, and we divide n by d repeatedly until it's no longer divisible. After handling 2, we iterate d from 3 to sqrt(n) in steps of 2 (odd numbers only). If after the loop n > 1, it's a prime factor itself. The time complexity is O(sqrt(n)) because we check up to sqrt(n) divisors, and space is O(k) for storing k factors (or O(1) if output is printed). This connects to L1 by using the optimized divisor checking as the key subroutine. Common mistakes include not handling n ≤ 1 properly, forgetting to update sqrt_n inside the loop, and incorrect handling of the final prime factor when n > 1 after the loop.

examples.trace:
n = 28, factors = []
Handle factor 2: 28 % 2 == 0 → factors.append(2), n = 28/2 = 14
14 % 2 == 0 → factors.append(2), n = 14/2 = 7
7 % 2 != 0 → done with 2
sqrt_n = floor(sqrt(7)) ≈ 2, d = 3 (start odd loop), but 3 > sqrt_n (2) → loop ends
n = 7 > 1 → factors.append(7), n = 1
Result: [2, 2, 7]

n = 315, factors = []
Handle factor 2: 315 % 2 != 0 → skip
sqrt_n = floor(sqrt(315)) ≈ 17, d = 3
315 % 3 == 0 → factors.append(3), n = 315/3 = 105
105 % 3 == 0 → factors.append(3), n = 105/3 = 35
35 % 3 != 0 → d = 5
35 % 5 == 0 → factors.append(5), n = 35/5 = 7
7 % 5 != 0 → d = 7
7 % 7 == 0 → factors.append(7), n = 7/7 = 1
Result: [3, 3, 5, 7]

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Optimized Divisor Checking Loop
ladders.desc: The core loop that checks odd divisors from 3 to sqrt(n) after handling factor 2.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine implements the optimized trial division loop, which is the heart of the factorization algorithm. The intuition is that after extracting all factors of 2, we only need to check odd potential divisors because all other primes are odd. For a number n, if it has a composite factor, it must have a prime factor ≤ sqrt(n). Thus, we iterate d from 3 to floor(sqrt(n)) in steps of 2. For each d, while n % d == 0, we record d as a factor and divide n by d. The loop condition d * d ≤ n ensures we only check up to sqrt(n), and we update this condition dynamically as n decreases. Time complexity is O(sqrt(n)) in worst case (when n is prime), and space is O(1) for the loop variables. This connects to L0 by being the main loop in the full solution, and to L2 as it relies on the insight of checking only up to sqrt(n). Common mistakes include not updating the loop condition after dividing n, or using a fixed sqrt_n variable that doesn't reflect the reduced n.

examples.trace:
Start: n=45, factors=[]
d=3: 45%3==0 → factors.append(3), n=45/3=15
15%3==0 → factors.append(3), n=15/3=5
5%3!=0 → d=5
5%5==0 → factors.append(5), n=5/5=1
Loop ends as d*d=25 > n=1
Result: factors=[3,3,5], n=1

Start: n=17, factors=[]
d=3: 17%3!=0, d*d=9 ≤ 17 → d=5
17%5!=0, d*d=25 > 17 → loop ends
n=17 > 1 → in full solution, would add 17 as factor
Result: factors=[], n=17

ladders.title: Core Logic — Check Divisors Only Up to sqrt(n)
ladders.desc: The insight that for factorization, we only need to test divisors up to the square root of n.
ladders.explanation: This core logic is based on the mathematical property that if n is composite, it must have at least one prime factor ≤ sqrt(n). The intuition: suppose n = a * b, with a ≤ b. Then a ≤ sqrt(n) because if both a and b were > sqrt(n), their product would exceed n. Therefore, to find all prime factors, we only need to check potential divisors up to floor(sqrt(n)). This reduces the number of checks from O(n) to O(sqrt(n)), making trial division feasible for larger numbers. The algorithm involves iterating i from 2 to sqrt(n) (or optimized as in L1). For each i, while n % i == 0, i is a factor. After the loop, if n > 1, it's a prime factor. Time complexity is O(sqrt(n)), space O(1). This connects to L1 as the basis for the loop bound, and to L3 which handles the divisibility check. Common mistakes include using i ≤ n instead of i * i ≤ n, or not handling the final prime factor.

examples.trace:
sqrt(36)=6
Check i=2: 36%2==0 → factor 2, n=18; 18%2==0 → factor 2, n=9; 9%2!=0
i=3: 9%3==0 → factor 3, n=3; 3%3==0 → factor 3, n=1
i=4: skip as 4>6? No, but 1%4!=0 and n=1, loop ends
i=5,6: similar
After loop, n=1 → done
Factors found: 2,2,3,3

sqrt(29)≈5.38, floor=5
Check i=2: 29%2!=0
i=3: 29%3!=0
i=4: 29%4!=0
i=5: 29%5!=0
Loop ends, n=29>1 → 29 is prime factor
Factors: 29

ladders.title: Building Block — Divisibility Check and Repeated Division
ladders.desc: Checking if a number is divisible by another and dividing repeatedly to extract all factors.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves two operations: checking divisibility using the modulo operator (%), and repeatedly dividing a number by a divisor to extract all occurrences of that factor. The intuition is that if n % d == 0, then d is a factor of n. To find all powers of d in n, we divide n by d as long as n remains divisible by d. This is crucial because a prime factor may appear multiple times (e.g., in 8 = 2³). The algorithm: while n % d == 0, append d to factors list and set n = n // d. Time complexity per divisor is O(log_d(n)) in worst case, but overall dominated by the sqrt(n) loop. Space is O(1) for the operation. This connects to L2 as it's used within the sqrt(n) loop, and to L4 which is the modulo operation. Common mistakes include not using integer division, or not updating n correctly in the loop.

examples.trace:
Start: n=72, d=2
72 % 2 == 0 → factors.append(2), n=72/2=36
36 % 2 == 0 → factors.append(2), n=36/2=18
18 % 2 == 0 → factors.append(2), n=18/2=9
9 % 2 != 0 → stop
Result: factors=[2,2,2], n=9

Start: n=125, d=5
125 % 5 == 0 → factors.append(5), n=125/5=25
25 % 5 == 0 → factors.append(5), n=25/5=5
5 % 5 == 0 → factors.append(5), n=5/5=1
1 % 5 != 0 → stop
Result: factors=[5,5,5], n=1

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Modulo and Integer Division
ladders.desc: Fundamental arithmetic operations: modulo (%) for remainder and integer division (//) for quotient.
ladders.explanation: These are basic arithmetic operations essential for factorization. Modulo (n % d) returns the remainder when n is divided by d. If n % d == 0, d divides n evenly. Integer division (n // d) returns the quotient as an integer, discarding any remainder. The intuition: in factorization, we use modulo to test divisibility, and integer division to reduce n after extracting a factor. For example, to check if 15 is divisible by 3: 15 % 3 == 0, so yes. Then 15 // 3 = 5 reduces n. Time and space are O(1) per operation. This connects to L3 as it's used in the divisibility check and repeated division. Common mistakes include using float division (/) instead of integer division, or misunderstanding modulo with negative numbers (but here n and d are positive).

examples.trace:
10 divided by 3: quotient = 3, remainder = 1 because 3*3=9, 10-9=1
So 10 % 3 = 1, 10 // 3 = 3

20 divided by 4: quotient = 5, remainder = 0 because 4*5=20, 20-20=0
So 20 % 4 = 0, 20 // 4 = 5

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Prime Numbers and Factors
ladders.desc: Understanding what prime numbers and factors are, as the foundational concept for factorization.
ladders.explanation: This is the most basic prerequisite: understanding prime numbers and factors. A prime number is a positive integer greater than 1 that has no positive divisors other than 1 and itself (e.g., 2, 3, 5, 7). A factor (or divisor) of a number n is an integer that divides n without leaving a remainder. For example, factors of 12 are 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 12. Prime factorization is expressing a number as a product of prime factors (e.g., 12 = 2 × 2 × 3). The intuition: every integer greater than 1 can be uniquely factorized into primes (Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic). This is foundational because the problem aims to find these prime factors. No algorithm here—just conceptual understanding. Time and space are not applicable. This connects to L4 as modulo checks divisibility to find factors. Common mistakes include thinking 1 is prime (it's not), or missing that prime factorization only uses prime numbers.

examples.trace: 6 can be divided by 2: 6 ÷ 2 = 3. 3 is prime. So prime factors are 2 and 3. Check: 2 × 3 = 6.
15 can be divided by 3: 15 ÷ 3 = 5. 5 is prime. So prime factors are 3 and 5. Check: 3 × 5 = 15.

approaches.name: Approach 3: Sieve of Eratosthenes Precomputation O(n log log n) precomputation, O(log n) per query
approaches.summary: Precompute smallest prime factors for all numbers up to n using a sieve, then repeatedly divide n by its smallest prime factor. This is efficient for multiple queries but requires O(n) space.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n log log n) for precomputation, O(log n) for factorization

ladders.title: Full Problem — Prime Factorization Using Sieve Precomputation
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of prime factorization using precomputed smallest prime factors via Sieve of Eratosthenes.
ladders.explanation: This approach solves prime factorization efficiently for multiple queries by separating the problem into two phases: precomputation and query processing. The intuition is that by precomputing the smallest prime factor (SPF) for every number up to a maximum limit using the Sieve of Eratosthenes, we can factorize any number within that range in logarithmic time. The sieve works by iteratively marking multiples of primes, ensuring each composite number gets its smallest prime factor recorded. For factorization, we repeatedly divide the number by its SPF from the precomputed array until it becomes 1, collecting all prime factors in the process. This is optimal for scenarios with many factorization queries since the O(n log log n) precomputation cost is amortized. The space complexity is O(n) for storing SPF array. Connection to L1: This full solution integrates the SPF array generation (L1) with the factorization loop. Common mistakes include not handling n=1 correctly (should return empty list), forgetting to update n during division, or using the sieve beyond its precomputed range. The algorithm: 1. Precompute SPF array up to max_n using sieve. 2. For each query n: initialize empty list factors; while n > 1: get p = spf[n]; append p; n = n // p; return factors. Time: O(max_n log log max_n) precomputation, O(log n) per query. Space: O(max_n).

examples.trace: Precompute SPF up to 100: spf[2]=2, spf[3]=3, spf[4]=2, spf[5]=5, spf[6]=2, etc. For n=60: spf[60]=2 → factors=[2], n=30; spf[30]=2 → factors=[2,2], n=15; spf[15]=3 → factors=[2,2,3], n=5; spf[5]=5 → factors=[2,2,3,5], n=1; stop.
Precompute SPF up to 20: spf[17]=17. For n=17: spf[17]=17 → factors=[17], n=1; stop.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Generating Smallest Prime Factor Array via Sieve
ladders.desc: Implement the Sieve of Eratosthenes to precompute smallest prime factor for all numbers up to n.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine creates an array spf where spf[i] stores the smallest prime factor of i. The intuition is that by iterating from 2 to n, we can efficiently mark composites and record their smallest prime factor. The algorithm initializes spf[i]=i for all i (assuming each number is prime initially). Then, for each i from 2 to sqrt(n), if spf[i]==i (i is prime), we mark its multiples: for j from i*i to n step i, if spf[j]==j (not yet marked), set spf[j]=i. This ensures each composite gets its smallest prime factor because we process primes in increasing order. Time complexity is O(n log log n) due to harmonic series of primes. Space is O(n). Connection to L0: This array enables fast factorization in L0. Common mistakes include starting j from i*i (not 2*i) to avoid redundant marking, or not iterating i up to sqrt(n). The pseudocode: initialize spf array of size n+1; for i from 0 to n: spf[i]=i; for i from 2 to sqrt(n): if spf[i]==i: for j from i*i to n step i: if spf[j]==j: spf[j]=i; return spf.

examples.trace: Initialize spf[i]=i. i=2: mark 4,6,8,10,12,14 → spf=2. i=3: mark 9,12,15 → spf=3 (but 12 already 2, so skip). i up to sqrt(15)≈3. Final spf as above.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Repeated Division Using Smallest Prime Factor
ladders.desc: Factorize a single number by repeatedly dividing by its smallest prime factor from a precomputed array.
ladders.explanation: This core logic demonstrates how to use a precomputed smallest prime factor (SPF) array to factorize a number n. The intuition is that every composite number can be broken down by its smallest prime factor, and repeating this process on the quotient eventually reduces n to 1, revealing all prime factors in increasing order. The algorithm assumes an existing spf array (from L1). It works by: while n > 1, get p = spf[n], append p to factors list, then update n = n // p. This loop runs until n becomes 1, at which point all prime factors have been collected. Time complexity is O(log n) because each division reduces n by at least a factor of 2 (since smallest prime factor is at least 2). Space is O(log n) for storing factors. Connection to L1: This uses the spf array generated in L1. Common mistakes include infinite loop if spf[n] is not properly defined (e.g., for n=1), or not handling large n that might exceed array bounds. The pseudocode: factors = []; while n > 1: p = spf[n]; factors.append(p); n = n // p; return factors.

examples.input: n=18, spf from L1 example for n=20
examples.trace: Given spf[18]=2 → factors=[2], n=9; spf[9]=3 → factors=[2,3], n=3; spf[3]=3 → factors=[2,3,3], n=1; stop.

examples.input: n=7, spf from L1 example for n=10
examples.trace: Given spf[7]=7 → factors=[7], n=1; stop.

ladders.title: Building Block — Sieve of Eratosthenes for Prime Checking
ladders.desc: Implement the classic Sieve of Eratosthenes to find all primes up to n.
ladders.explanation: This building block is a simplified version of the sieve used in L1, focusing on generating a boolean array is_prime where is_prime[i] indicates if i is prime. The intuition is to iteratively mark multiples of primes as composite, starting from 2. The algorithm initializes is_prime array of size n+1 with all true except 0 and 1. Then, for i from 2 to sqrt(n), if is_prime[i] is true, mark all multiples j from i*i to n as false. This works because any composite number has a prime factor ≤ sqrt(n). Time complexity is O(n log log n), space O(n). Connection to L2: This sieve is the foundation for the SPF sieve in L1, which extends it to record smallest prime factors. Common mistakes include not optimizing by starting j from i*i, or iterating i beyond sqrt(n). The pseudocode: initialize is_prime array of size n+1 with true; is_prime[0]=false; is_prime[1]=false; for i from 2 to sqrt(n): if is_prime[i]: for j from i*i to n step i: is_prime[j]=false; return is_prime.

examples.output: [False, False, True, True, False, True, False, True, False, False, False]
examples.trace: Initialize is_prime all true except 0,1 false. i=2 (prime): mark 4,6,8,10 false. i=3 (prime): mark 9 false. i up to sqrt(10)≈3. Final: 0:F,1:F,2:T,3:T,4:F,5:T,6:F,7:T,8:F,9:F,10:F.
Initialize. i=2: mark 4,6,8,10,12,14,16,18,20 false. i=3: mark 9,12,15,18 false. i=5: mark 25>20, skip. i up to sqrt(20)≈4. Result: is_prime true for 2,3,5,7,11,13,17,19.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Integer Division and Looping
ladders.desc: Perform repeated integer division in a loop to break down a number.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves using a while loop to repeatedly divide a number by a fixed divisor until a condition is met. The intuition is that division reduces the number's magnitude, and looping allows us to extract multiple factors. For example, to count how many times a number n is divisible by 2, we can do: while n % 2 == 0: n = n // 2; count++. This demonstrates the pattern used in L2 for factorization. Time complexity depends on the number of divisions, O(log n) in worst case. Space is O(1). Connection to L3: This operation is a simpler version of the division in L2, which uses dynamic divisors from SPF array. Common mistakes include not using integer division (// instead of / in Python), or infinite loops if the condition never becomes false. The pseudocode: initialize count=0; while n % divisor == 0: n = n // divisor; count++; return count, n.

examples.trace: n=17, 17%3!=0, so loop never runs, count=0, n=17.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Prime Numbers and Divisibility
ladders.desc: Understand prime numbers, composite numbers, and basic divisibility checks.
ladders.explanation: This foundation covers the fundamental concepts needed for prime factorization: prime numbers, composite numbers, and how to check divisibility. A prime number is a natural number greater than 1 that has no positive divisors other than 1 and itself (e.g., 2,3,5,7). A composite number has at least one divisor other than 1 and itself (e.g., 4,6,8,9). Divisibility means that when integer a is divided by integer b (b≠0), the remainder is 0; we say b divides a. To check if a number n is prime, we can test divisibility by all integers from 2 to sqrt(n); if any divide n, it's composite. The intuition is that factors come in pairs, so checking up to sqrt(n) is sufficient. Time complexity for primality check is O(sqrt(n)). Space is O(1). Connection to L4: This concept underlies why division works in factorization. Common mistakes include forgetting that 1 is not prime, or not handling edge cases like n=0 or n=1. The pseudocode for primality check: if n < 2: return False; for i from 2 to sqrt(n): if n % i == 0: return False; return True.

examples.trace: n=7 >1. Check i=2: 7%2=1 ≠0; i=3: 7%3=1; sqrt(7)≈2.6, so stop. No divisors found, so prime.
n=9 >1. Check i=2: 9%2=1 ≠0; i=3: 9%3=0 → divisible, so composite.

approaches.name: Approach 4: Pollard's Rho Algorithm O(n^(1/4)) expected
approaches.summary: Use a randomized algorithm based on Floyd's cycle detection to find factors quickly for large composite numbers. It's probabilistic and more complex but handles very large inputs efficiently.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Prime Factorization using Pollard's Rho Algorithm
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of Pollard's Rho algorithm to factorize a number into prime factors.
ladders.explanation: Pollard's Rho algorithm is a probabilistic algorithm for integer factorization that works efficiently for large composite numbers. The intuition is based on the birthday paradox and Floyd's cycle detection. If we generate a pseudo-random sequence modulo n, by the birthday paradox, we expect a collision (two values equal modulo some factor p of n) after about O(√p) steps. Once we find such a collision, we can compute gcd(x-y, n) to potentially extract a non-trivial factor of n. The algorithm uses Floyd's cycle detection to find collisions without storing the entire sequence, making it memory-efficient. For the full problem, we combine Pollard's Rho with trial division for small primes and Miller-Rabin primality testing to recursively factor composite numbers until all factors are prime. The expected time complexity is O(n^(1/4)) for finding a factor, and overall it's efficient for large numbers. Connection to L1: This level integrates the Pollard's Rho factor-finding subroutine (L1) with primality testing and recursive factorization. Common mistakes include not handling small primes efficiently, which can slow down the algorithm, and improper random function choice, which might lead to failure. Always use a good pseudo-random function like f(x) = (x² + c) mod n with appropriate c.

examples.trace: Step 1: Check if n is prime using Miller-Rabin (not shown in detail here). n=8051 is composite. Step 2: Use Pollard's Rho to find a factor. Initialize x=2, y=2, d=1, c=1. While d==1: x = (x*x + 1) % 8051 = (4+1)%8051=5. y = (y*y + 1) % 8051 twice: first y=(4+1)%8051=5, then y=(25+1)%8051=26. d = gcd(|x-y|, n) = gcd(|5-26|, 8051)=gcd(21,8051)=1. Continue: x=(25+1)%8051=26. y: compute twice: first (26*26+1)%8051=(676+1)%8051=677, then (677*677+1)%8051=(458329+1)%8051=458330%8051=458330-8051*56=458330-450856=7474, then (7474*7474+1)%8051 is large, but eventually d becomes non-1. After several iterations, find d=97. Step 3: Factor 8051/97=83. Step 4: Check 97 and 83 are prime. Output [83, 97] (sorted).

Step 1: n=123456789 is composite. Step 2: Use trial division for small primes up to 1000: find 3 divides n, so factor out 3: n=41152263, then 3 again: n=13717421. Step 3: Now n=13717421, use Pollard's Rho. Initialize x=2, y=2, d=1, c=1. Iterate: x=(4+1)%13717421=5, y compute twice: first 5, then (25+1)%13717421=26, d=gcd(21,13717421)=1. Continue iterations until d=3607. Step 4: Factor 13717421/3607=3803. Step 5: Check 3607 and 3803 are prime. Output factors: [3, 3, 3607, 3803].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Pollard's Rho Factor Finding
ladders.desc: Implement the Pollard's Rho algorithm to find a single non-trivial factor of a composite number.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the core of Pollard's Rho algorithm for factorization. The intuition is to detect a cycle in a pseudo-random sequence modulo n, which indicates a collision modulo some factor p of n. We use Floyd's cycle detection algorithm with two pointers (x and y) moving at different speeds through the sequence defined by f(x) = (x² + c) mod n, where c is a random constant. When x ≡ y (mod p) for some factor p, then gcd(|x-y|, n) will be a multiple of p, potentially revealing a non-trivial factor. The algorithm is probabilistic and may need multiple attempts with different c values. Time complexity is O(n^(1/4)) expected to find a factor, and space is O(1). Connection to L0: This subroutine is called recursively in the full factorization algorithm to break down composite numbers. Common mistakes include not randomizing c properly, which can lead to infinite loops, and not handling the case when gcd is n (i.e., no factor found). Always check if the factor found is non-trivial (not 1 or n).

examples.trace: Initialize x=2, y=2, d=1. Iteration 1: x=(4+1)%561=5. y: f(2)=5, f(5)=(25+1)%561=26. d=gcd(|5-26|,561)=gcd(21,561). Compute gcd: 561 mod 21=21*26=546, remainder 15, 21 mod 15=6, 15 mod 6=3, 6 mod 3=0, so gcd=3. Since d!=1, break and output d=3.

testCases.expected: 104729 (prime, so may return n or need multiple c)

ladders.title: Core Logic — Floyd's Cycle Detection in a Sequence
ladders.desc: Implement Floyd's cycle detection algorithm to find a collision in a sequence generated by a function f.
ladders.explanation: Floyd's cycle detection algorithm, also known as the tortoise and hare algorithm, is used to detect cycles in a sequence. The intuition is to use two pointers, one moving slowly (tortoise) and one moving fast (hare). If there is a cycle, the fast pointer will eventually meet the slow pointer inside the cycle. For Pollard's Rho, we apply this to the sequence x_i defined by x_{i+1} = f(x_i) mod n, where f is a pseudo-random function. The slow pointer x moves one step per iteration (x = f(x)), and the fast pointer y moves two steps per iteration (y = f(f(y))). When x ≡ y (mod p) for some factor p of n, we have found a collision. The algorithm runs in O(μ + λ) time, where μ is the start of the cycle and λ is the cycle length, but in Pollard's Rho, we expect this to be O(√p). Space is O(1). Connection to L1: This cycle detection is the mechanism used in Pollard's Rho to find factors without storing the entire sequence. Common mistakes include not updating pointers correctly or infinite loops if no cycle exists; in Pollard's Rho, cycles are expected due to the birthday paradox.

examples.trace: Sequence: x0=2. x1=f(2)=(4+1) mod 10=5. x2=f(5)=(25+1) mod 10=6. x3=f(6)=(36+1) mod 10=7. x4=f(7)=(49+1) mod 10=0. x5=f(0)=(0+1) mod 10=1. x6=f(1)=(1+1) mod 10=2. So sequence cycles: 2,5,6,7,0,1,2,... Use Floyd: tortoise x=2, hare y=2. Step 1: x=f(2)=5, y=f(f(2))=f(5)=6. Not equal. Step 2: x=f(5)=6, y=f(f(6))=f(7)=0. Not equal. Step 3: x=f(6)=7, y=f(f(0))=f(1)=2. Not equal. Step 4: x=f(7)=0, y=f(f(1))=f(2)=5. Not equal. Step 5: x=f(0)=1, y=f(f(5))=f(6)=7. Not equal. Step 6: x=f(1)=2, y=f(f(6))=f(7)=0. Not equal. Step 7: x=f(2)=5, y=f(f(7))=f(0)=1. Not equal. Step 8: x=f(5)=6, y=f(f(0))=f(1)=2. Not equal. Step 9: x=f(6)=7, y=f(f(1))=f(2)=5. Not equal. Step 10: x=f(7)=0, y=f(f(2))=f(5)=6. Not equal. Eventually, they meet; in this sequence, they meet when both are in the cycle. For simplicity, assume they meet at x=y=2 after full cycle detection.

Sequence: 1,4,2,0,3,1,... (cycles). Floyd: x=1, y=1. Step 1: x=f(1)=4, y=f(f(1))=f(4)=2. Not equal. Step 2: x=f(4)=2, y=f(f(2))=f(0)=3. Not equal. Step 3: x=f(2)=0, y=f(f(0))=f(3)=1. Not equal. Step 4: x=f(0)=3, y=f(f(3))=f(1)=4. Not equal. Step 5: x=f(3)=1, y=f(f(1))=f(4)=2. Not equal. Step 6: x=f(1)=4, y=f(f(4))=f(2)=0. Not equal. Continue until they meet; in this linear sequence mod prime, it may not cycle until full period, but Floyd will detect when x=y after enough steps.

testCases.expected: Cycle or no cycle depending on definition

ladders.title: Building Block — Greatest Common Divisor (GCD) Computation
ladders.desc: Compute the greatest common divisor of two integers using the Euclidean algorithm.
ladders.explanation: The greatest common divisor (GCD) of two integers a and b is the largest positive integer that divides both a and b without a remainder. The Euclidean algorithm is an efficient method to compute GCD based on the principle that gcd(a, b) = gcd(b, a mod b). The intuition is that any common divisor of a and b also divides a mod b, so we can reduce the problem recursively until b becomes 0, at which point a is the GCD. This algorithm runs in O(log min(a, b)) time, making it very fast. Space complexity is O(1) for the iterative version. Connection to L2: In Pollard's Rho, GCD is used to check if |x-y| shares a non-trivial factor with n, which is key to finding factors. Common mistakes include not handling negative numbers (take absolute values) and infinite recursion if not reducing properly. Always ensure to compute a mod b correctly and swap parameters as needed.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Modular Arithmetic
ladders.desc: Perform addition, multiplication, and exponentiation modulo n.
ladders.explanation: Modular arithmetic involves operations on integers where results are taken modulo n, meaning we compute the remainder when divided by n. For example, a mod n is the remainder of a divided by n. Key operations include addition mod n: (a + b) mod n = (a mod n + b mod n) mod n; multiplication mod n: (a * b) mod n = (a mod n * b mod n) mod n; and exponentiation mod n can be done efficiently using binary exponentiation to avoid large intermediate values. The intuition is that working modulo n keeps numbers small and is essential in algorithms like Pollard's Rho to prevent overflow and detect collisions. Time complexity for basic operations is O(1) for addition and multiplication, and O(log exponent) for exponentiation. Space is O(1). Connection to L3: Modular arithmetic is used in the Euclidean algorithm for GCD (e.g., a mod b) and in Pollard's Rho for the random function f(x) = (x² + c) mod n. Common mistakes include not reducing mod n at each step, which can cause overflow, and misunderstanding negative mod results (use (a mod n + n) mod n for negatives).

examples.trace: Step 1: 123 mod 7 = 123 - 7*17 = 123 - 119 = 4. Step 2: 456 mod 7 = 456 - 7*65 = 456 - 455 = 1. Step 3: (4 + 1) mod 7 = 5 mod 7 = 5. But wait, check: 123+456=579, 579 mod 7: 7*82=574, remainder 5. Yes, output 5. Correction: output should be 5.
Step 1: 15 mod 11 = 4. Step 2: 23 mod 11 = 1. Step 3: (4 * 1) mod 11 = 4 mod 11 = 4. But check: 15*23=345, 345 mod 11: 11*31=341, remainder 4. Yes, output 4. Correction: output should be 4.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Integer Division and Remainder
ladders.desc: Divide two integers and compute the quotient and remainder.
ladders.explanation: Integer division involves dividing one integer a (dividend) by another integer b (divisor, b ≠ 0) to get a quotient q and remainder r such that a = b * q + r, where 0 ≤ r < |b|. The remainder is also called a mod b. This is a fundamental concept in arithmetic and is crucial for modular operations. The intuition is that division partitions a into equal groups of size b, with q being the number of full groups and r being the leftover. For example, 17 divided by 5 gives quotient 3 and remainder 2 because 17 = 5*3 + 2. In programming, this is often done using the // operator for quotient and % for remainder. Time complexity is O(1) for basic operations. Connection to L4: Integer division and remainder are the basis for modular arithmetic; for instance, a mod n is the remainder when a is divided by n. Common mistakes include dividing by zero, which is undefined, and misunderstanding negative remainders (in most programming languages, remainder has the same sign as the dividend, but in mathematics, we usually take non-negative remainder). Always ensure the remainder is non-negative for modular arithmetic.

examples.trace: Step 1: Divide 17 by 5. 5 fits into 17 three times because 5*3=15. Step 2: Subtract 15 from 17 to get remainder: 17 - 15 = 2. Step 3: Verify: 17 = 5*3 + 2, and 0 ≤ 2 < 5. So quotient q=3, remainder r=2.
Step 1: Divide 100 by 7. 7 fits into 100 fourteen times because 7*14=98. Step 2: Subtract 98 from 100 to get remainder: 100 - 98 = 2. Step 3: Verify: 100 = 7*14 + 2, and 0 ≤ 2 < 7. So q=14, r=2.
